refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement period and baseline, (2) what constitutes 'available' vs. 'unavailable' (e.g., does safety shutdown count as unavailability?), (3) the scope of the system (vehicle only, or full NGHE solution including teleoperation and support services?), and (4) the role of planned vs. unplanned downtime. High safety criticality demands that safety-driven shutdowns are tracked separately from reliability failures.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational logs from deployed systems over the agreed evaluation period (e.g., 12 months). Calculate availability as (Total Scheduled Time − Unplanned Downtime − Planned Maintenance Time) / Total Scheduled Time. Segregate root causes: mechanical failure, software fault, communication loss, safety intervention, operator error, environmental factors. Compare against target ≥0.97 with confidence interval.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall achieve ≥0.97 availability by minimizing unplanned mechanical and electrical failures.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall achieve ≥0.97 availability by maintaining reliable network connectivity and command/telemetry link integrity.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall achieve ≥0.97 availability by ensuring safety monitoring and intervention systems remain operational and responsive.

FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE Solution shall integrate availability data from all subsystems and provide real-time visibility of system health and downtime root causes to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundancy and fault tolerance across all critical subsystems (Vehicle propulsion, Teleoperation network, Safety sensors) with automated failover and graceful degradation; establish preventive maintenance schedules based on MTBF analysis; deploy real-time health monitoring and predictive diagnostics.

DP-B: Design the Vehicle with high-reliability components (MTBF >10,000 hours), implement network redundancy (dual communication links), and establish a centralized availability dashboard that aggregates uptime metrics from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services; use SLA-based service level agreements with external network providers.

DP-C: Establish a hierarchical availability architecture: (1) Vehicle-level availability through component redundancy and self-healing; (2) Teleoperation-level availability through network diversity and link failover; (3) Safety Services-level availability through independent monitoring channels; (4) System-level availability through orchestrated health management and operator notification protocols.

DP-D: Implement a modular, replaceable component strategy with quick-swap subsystems (e.g., battery packs, communication modules, sensor arrays) to minimize downtime; pair with on-site spare inventory and rapid logistics support.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Establish a hierarchical availability architecture with subsystem-level redundancy and failover (Vehicle propulsion and electrical systems), network diversity for Teleoperation (dual communication links with automatic switchover), independent Safety Services monitoring, and a centralized system-level health orchestration layer that aggregates telemetry, detects faults, triggers failover, and notifies operators and maintenance teams in real time.

potential_sources: Coupling between Vehicle subsystem availability (mechanical/electrical reliability) and Teleoperation subsystem availability (network link integrity): both must be operational for remote operation; however, the Vehicle can operate in autonomous or manual modes if Teleoperation fails, reducing coupling severity.

Coupling between Safety Services and Vehicle/Teleoperation: Safety Services must remain independent and operational even if Vehicle or Teleoperation fail; this is a decoupling requirement, not a coupling risk.

Coupling between the centralized health orchestration layer and individual subsystem monitoring: the orchestration layer depends on real-time telemetry from all subsystems; loss of telemetry from one subsystem should not cascade to others (requires isolation).
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is hierarchical and modular, allowing each subsystem to maintain availability independently while the orchestration layer coordinates failover and health visibility. The design minimizes coupling by allowing graceful degradation: if Teleoperation fails, the Vehicle can revert to autonomous or manual operation; if one communication link fails, the backup link takes over without affecting Vehicle operation. Safety Services is intentionally decoupled and independent.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Network redundancy (dual communication links) is technically and economically feasible for the deployment environment (e.g., cellular, satellite, or private radio networks are available).
Assumption: The Vehicle subsystem can operate safely in autonomous or manual modes if Teleoperation is unavailable.
Assumption: Preventive maintenance schedules can be coordinated with customer operational windows without excessive downtime.
Risk: Complexity of the hierarchical architecture may introduce new failure modes in the orchestration layer itself; requires rigorous testing and validation.
Risk: Network redundancy adds cost and weight; customer may not accept the trade-off.
Risk: Operator training and procedures must account for failover scenarios; inadequate training could negate availability gains.
Risk: Safety Services independence requires separate power, sensors, and communication channels; this increases system complexity and cost.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-C is sound but should be refined to explicitly define: (1) the failover decision logic and latency (e.g., how quickly does the system detect a failure and switch to backup?), (2) the role of predictive diagnostics and condition-based maintenance in extending availability, (3) the interface between the health orchestration layer and the operator/maintenance team (e.g., what alerts are sent, when, and to whom?), and (4) the acceptance criteria for 'availability' in the context of safety-driven shutdowns (e.g., if Safety Services triggers a shutdown, is that counted as unavailability or as a safety success?).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system integration module that owns the hierarchical availability architecture, including: (1) subsystem health monitoring and aggregation, (2) centralized orchestration logic for failover and degradation, (3) real-time availability dashboard and reporting, (4) integration of telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, and (5) operator/maintenance team notification and alerting. The NGHE Solution module does NOT own the Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services subsystems themselves, but rather orchestrates their availability and provides visibility.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound telemetry from Vehicle (propulsion status, electrical health, sensor data), Teleoperation (network link quality, latency, packet loss), and Safety Services (safety sensor status, intervention events). Used by NGHE Solution to assess subsystem health and availability.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound tasking commands from NGHE Solution to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to trigger failover, graceful degradation, or mode changes (e.g., switch from remote to autonomous operation). Also used to coordinate maintenance windows.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety alerts from NGHE Solution to Safety Services and operators when availability thresholds are breached or critical failures are detected. Also receives safety alerts from Safety Services to inform availability decisions (e.g., if Safety Services triggers a shutdown, NGHE Solution logs it as a safety-driven downtime event, not a reliability failure).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication link between NGHE Solution and external systems (Site Manager dashboard, Maintenance team, Tele-operator console). Used to transmit availability metrics, health status, and alerts; also receives operator commands and maintenance notifications.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of the hierarchical availability architecture and orchestration logic. It aggregates telemetry from all subsystems, makes failover decisions, and provides visibility to operators and maintenance teams. Containment is high because the availability KPI is a system-level concern that requires cross-subsystem coordination. Diffusion risk is low: the only diffused elements are the subsystem-specific health monitoring logic (e.g., Vehicle propulsion health checks), which should remain owned by the Vehicle module, but the aggregation and orchestration logic is cleanly contained in NGHE Solution.

Vehicle could own its own availability logic (e.g., propulsion redundancy, electrical fault tolerance), but it cannot own the system-level orchestration or cross-subsystem failover logic. If Vehicle were assigned the full DP, it would need to own Teleoperation and Safety Services availability as well, which violates separation of concerns. Vehicle is a candidate for owning subsystem-level availability (FR-NGHE-004.1), but not the top-level KPI.

Teleoperation could own network redundancy and link failover logic, but it cannot own Vehicle or Safety Services availability. Similar to Vehicle, Teleoperation is a candidate for owning subsystem-level availability (FR-NGHE-004.2), but not the top-level KPI. Assigning the full DP to Teleoperation would create diffusion across Vehicle and Safety Services.

Safety Services owns safety monitoring and intervention, but availability of the overall NGHE solution is not a safety-specific concern; it is a business/operational KPI. Assigning the full DP to Safety Services would create significant diffusion and confusion about the role of safety vs. operational availability. Safety Services should own FR-NGHE-004.3 (Safety Services subsystem availability), but not the top-level KPI.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the correct owner of CR-NGHE-004 (Availability KPI) because: (1) it is the only module with visibility into all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), (2) it owns the orchestration logic that coordinates failover and degradation across subsystems, (3) it owns the health monitoring and aggregation layer that measures and reports availability, and (4) it owns the interface to external stakeholders (Site Manager, Maintenance team, Tele-operator) who need visibility into system availability. The sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through 004.4) are allocated to the respective subsystems and the NGHE Solution integration layer.
containment_risks: Risk: If the NGHE Solution module is too large or poorly scoped, it could become a 'god module' that owns too much logic and becomes difficult to test and maintain. Mitigation: Define clear boundaries between NGHE Solution orchestration logic and subsystem-specific health monitoring logic.

Risk: The NGHE Solution module depends on real-time telemetry from all subsystems; if telemetry is delayed or lost, the orchestration logic may make incorrect failover decisions. Mitigation: Implement timeout and fallback logic; design the system to degrade gracefully if telemetry is unavailable.

Risk: The NGHE Solution module must coordinate with external systems (Site Manager dashboard, Maintenance team) to report availability and receive maintenance notifications; this introduces external dependencies that could affect availability. Mitigation: Design the NGHE Solution to operate independently of external systems; external reporting is a secondary concern.

Risk: Safety Services must remain independent of NGHE Solution orchestration; if NGHE Solution fails, Safety Services must continue to operate. Mitigation: Design Safety Services as a separate, independent module with its own power, sensors, and communication channels.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: The NGHE Solution module assignment is sound. However, the scope should be refined to explicitly separate: (1) the core orchestration logic (failover decision-making, health aggregation), which is owned by NGHE Solution, and (2) the subsystem-specific health monitoring logic (e.g., Vehicle propulsion health checks, Teleoperation link quality assessment), which is owned by the respective subsystems. The NGHE Solution should consume health status from subsystems via the Telemetry interface and issue commands via the Tasking interface, but should not own the detailed health monitoring algorithms for each subsystem. This separation ensures that NGHE Solution remains focused on system-level orchestration and does not become diffused across subsystem-specific concerns.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR (CR-NGHE-004) clearly defines the availability target (≥0.97) and the measurement approach (ratio of available time to scheduled time). The selected DP (hierarchical availability architecture with subsystem-level redundancy and failover) directly addresses the FR by: (1) reducing unplanned downtime through redundancy and fault tolerance, (2) enabling graceful degradation so that loss of one subsystem does not cascade to others, (3) providing real-time health visibility to enable rapid response to failures, and (4) supporting preventive maintenance to extend MTBF. The DP is logically sound and feasible. No coupling issues detected that would prevent implementation.

diffusion_risks: Low diffusion risk: The NGHE Solution module cleanly owns the orchestration logic and health aggregation, while subsystem-specific health monitoring remains owned by Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. The interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimize coupling.

Moderate diffusion risk: The NGHE Solution module depends on real-time telemetry from all subsystems; if telemetry is delayed or lost, the orchestration logic may be impaired. This is a design risk, not an ownership risk, and can be mitigated through timeout and fallback logic.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The NGHE Solution module has a containment score of 0.95, indicating strong alignment between the DP and the IM boundary. The module owns the core orchestration logic and health aggregation, which are the key elements of the DP. Subsystem-specific health monitoring is appropriately delegated to the respective subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), reducing diffusion. The external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are all known interfaces and are correctly assigned.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: The refined FR is measurable and verifiable: operational logs from deployed systems can be collected and analyzed to calculate availability as (Total Scheduled Time − Unplanned Downtime − Planned Maintenance Time) / Total Scheduled Time. Root causes can be segregated (mechanical failure, software fault, communication loss, safety intervention, operator error, environmental factors) to support continuous improvement. The NGHE Solution module owns the health monitoring and reporting logic, so it can provide the data needed for verification.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of CR-NGHE-004 to the NGHE Solution module is clear and unambiguous. The sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through 004.4) are allocated to the respective subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and the NGHE Solution integration layer. Each sub-FR is atomic and can be assigned to a specific module owner. The interfaces between modules are well-defined (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link), enabling clear traceability from FR to DP to IM.

recommended_revisions: Refine the definition of 'availability' to explicitly account for safety-driven shutdowns: are they counted as unavailability, or as a separate category? This affects how the availability KPI is measured and reported.
Define the failover decision logic and latency: how quickly does the system detect a failure and switch to backup? What is the acceptable latency for failover?

Define the role of predictive diagnostics and condition-based maintenance in extending availability: should the NGHE Solution module own predictive analytics, or should this be delegated to the Maintenance team?

Define the acceptance criteria for 'availability' in the context of planned maintenance: should planned maintenance windows be excluded from the availability calculation, or included as planned downtime?

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that requires decomposition into subsystem-level FRs and DPs. The refined FR and selected DP are sound and well-aligned with the NGHE Solution module. However, the DP is not atomic and requires further decomposition to define the specific design parameters for each subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and the NGHE Solution integration layer. The sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through 004.4) are ready for the next level of decomposition, where each sub-FR will be refined, a DP will be selected, and an IM will be assigned. This hierarchical decomposition will ensure that availability is addressed at all levels of the system architecture.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The Vehicle subsystem shall achieve ≥0.97 availability by minimizing unplanned mechanical and electrical failures through redundancy, fault tolerance, and preventive maintenance.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The Teleoperation subsystem shall achieve ≥0.97 availability by maintaining reliable network connectivity and command/telemetry link integrity through network redundancy and failover.

FR-NGHE-004.3: The Safety Services subsystem shall achieve ≥0.97 availability by ensuring safety monitoring and intervention systems remain operational and responsive, independent of Vehicle and Teleoperation availability.

FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE Solution shall integrate availability data from all subsystems and provide real-time visibility of system health and downtime root causes to the Site Manager and Maintenance team through a centralized health orchestration layer.

assumptions: Network redundancy (dual communication links) is technically and economically feasible for the deployment environment.
The Vehicle subsystem can operate safely in autonomous or manual modes if Teleoperation is unavailable.
Preventive maintenance schedules can be coordinated with customer operational windows without excessive downtime.
Safety Services can be designed as an independent subsystem with separate power, sensors, and communication channels.
The customer-defined availability target is ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period (e.g., 12 months).
Operational logs and telemetry data are available for post-deployment verification of availability.
open_risks: Complexity of the hierarchical architecture may introduce new failure modes in the orchestration layer; requires rigorous testing and validation.
Network redundancy adds cost and weight; customer may not accept the trade-off.
Operator training and procedures must account for failover scenarios; inadequate training could negate availability gains.
Safety Services independence requires separate power, sensors, and communication channels; this increases system complexity and cost.
Failover decision logic and latency must be carefully designed to avoid cascading failures or false positives.
Predictive diagnostics and condition-based maintenance require data analytics capabilities that may not be available in all deployment environments.
evidence_needed: MTBF (Mean Time Between Failure) data for Vehicle subsystem components (propulsion, electrical, sensors).
Network reliability data for available communication links (cellular, satellite, private radio) in the deployment environment.
Safety Services failure modes and effects analysis (FMEA) to ensure independence from Vehicle and Teleoperation.
Operator training and procedure documentation to support failover scenarios.
Cost-benefit analysis of network redundancy and other availability-enhancing features.
Post-deployment operational logs and telemetry data to verify availability KPI achievement.
Customer acceptance criteria for 'availability' in the context of safety-driven shutdowns and planned maintenance.
